1: Bildad’s second speech begins with a rhetorical challenge to Job to let the speech end so the friends can speak. In the passion narrative, the crowds similarly shouted down the voice of the innocent sufferer (Mark 15:13–14: they cried out even more, “crucify him”; Pilate tried to argue but was shouted down). The pressure to silence the one who insists on innocence in the face of conventional judgment is a constant feature of the innocent sufferer’s situation.
2: Trapping with words before understanding (“how long will you hunt for words”) uses the hunting metaphor for Bildad’s argumentative strategy. In the passion narrative, the Pharisees and Herodians similarly “sent to him some of their disciples and some of the Herodians to entangle him in his talk” (Matthew 22:15–16). The trap-setting opponents who look for the damaging word are a constant feature of the innocent sufferer’s legal persecution.
3: Why are we counted as cattle and stupid in Job’s sight? — Bildad’s wounded pride at being called unwise by the sufferer he is supposedly helping. The religious authorities similarly resented Jesus’ implicit and explicit critique of their wisdom: Luke 20:19 (“the scribes and the chief priests sought to lay hands on him at that very hour, for they perceived that he had told this parable against them”). The reversal of wisdom-assessment — the supposedly wise calling the sufferer stupid while the sufferer correctly evaluates the wise as cattle-dumb — is the structural irony of both Job and the passion.
4: Job in tearing himself in his anger — shall the earth be forsaken for you, and the rock be removed out of its place? — describes the friends’ accusation that Job’s suffering is self-inflicted. At the cross, the accusation that Jesus’ suffering was self-caused (“he saved others; let him save himself,” Luke 23:35) is the same logic. The “rock removed from its place” anticipates the stone that was rolled away from the tomb (Matthew 28:2) — the rock that the friends said could not be moved for Job’s sake was moved at Christ’s resurrection.
5: The wicked man’s light goes out (gam ’or resha’im yid’ak) and his flame does not shine. Matthew 27:45: “now from the sixth hour there was darkness over all the land until the ninth hour.” The darkness at the crucifixion was the cosmic sign of divine judgment falling upon the sin-bearer; the light going out is both the literal darkness of the three hours and the symbolic judgment on the sin that Christ bore. Bildad describes the fate of the wicked; the crucifixion darkness shows this judgment falling on Christ who bore the wicked’s penalty.
6: The light in his tent grows dark and his lamp above him is put out — the extinction of the domestic light as the symbol of hope and presence. John 8:12: “I am the light of the world. Whoever follows me will not walk in darkness, but will have the light of life.” The progressive extinction of the wicked man’s light is the anti-type of Christ, the light that darkness cannot extinguish. The lamp put out in judgment on the wicked is the same darkness that Christ enters voluntarily — and conquers.
7: The vigorous stride of the wicked shortening, his own counsel throwing him down, describes the self-destruction of the proud. Proverbs 16:18: “pride goes before destruction, and a haughty spirit before a fall.” The structural contrast with Christ: his humility (“I am gentle and lowly in heart,” Matthew 11:29) is the anti-type of the wicked one’s shortened stride. Christ’s deliberate lowering of himself (Philippians 2:7–8) is the voluntary inversion of the wicked man’s pride-before-fall.
8: The wicked man walking into a net, stepping into a web — the accumulation of traps. In the passion narrative, the arrest is framed as a capture: Mark 14:44 (the signal to arrest Jesus, “seize him”); Luke 22:54 (“they seized him and led him away”). But the irony of the arrest is that the one who was “seized” had already freely submitted: John 10:18 (“no one takes it from me, but I lay it down of my own accord”). The trap that catches the wicked is entered voluntarily by Christ as the necessary path through death.
9: A snare seizing by the heel (yahazez be’aqev) and a trap holding him fast uses the heel imagery that reaches back to Genesis 3:15 (the serpent striking the heel of the woman’s offspring). The messianic promise is that the seed of the woman will be struck in the heel but will crush the serpent’s head. The “heel-seizing” (’aqev, the root of “Jacob”/supplanter) that catches the wicked is, in the messianic reading, the wound Christ received at the cross — the fatal blow that was not ultimately fatal, because the head-crusher prevailed.
10: The rope hidden in the ground for the wicked, the trap in the path, continues the multi-layered snare imagery. In the passion narrative, the plot against Jesus was hidden and multi-layered: John 11:53 (“from that day on they made plans to put him to death”); the betrayal of Judas was the specific trap in the path (Luke 22:3–6: “Satan entered into Judas…and he went away and conferred with the chief priests and officers how he might betray him”). The hidden rope and the path-trap were the mechanisms of the passion.
11: Terrors frightening the wicked on every side (sevivav biehatu ballahot) scattering at his feet describes the psychological state of the wicked at judgment. Gethsemane inverts this: it is Christ who is surrounded by terrors (“he began to be sorrowful and troubled…my soul is very sorrowful, even to death,” Matthew 26:37–38). Christ voluntarily entered the terrors that belong to the guilty; the terrors that scatter the wicked at their feet are the terrors Christ bore in the garden before the cross.
12: Calamity hungry for the wicked and disaster ready at his side describes the constant attendance of doom. In the passion, “this is the hour” (John 12:27; Luke 22:53) was the moment when the calamity that had been attending arrived. Christ knew the hour was coming and moved deliberately toward it: John 12:23 (“the hour has come for the Son of Man to be glorified”). The calamity that is “hungry” for the wicked is the judgment that was satisfied at Calvary by the one who took it voluntarily.
13: Eating the parts of his skin, eating his limbs, the firstborn of death devouring him — disease consuming the body. The “firstborn of death” (bekhoro mavet) is a personification of the most lethal disease or, typologically, death’s primary offspring. Hebrews 2:14–15: Christ “through death destroyed the one who has the power of death, that is, the devil, and delivered all those who through fear of death were subject to lifelong slavery.” The firstborn of death that devours the wicked is the power that Christ’s death destroyed; his resurrection was the definitive defeat of the firstborn of death.
14: “He is torn from the tent in which he trusted and is marched before the king of terrors” (malkey ballahot) — the king of terrors is the personification of death. 1 Corinthians 15:55: “O death, where is your victory? O death, where is your sting?” (quoting Hosea 13:14). The king of terrors before whom the wicked is marched is the death that Christ faced on behalf of the wicked; Colossians 2:15: “he disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him.” The triumphant march before the king of terrors is, in Christ’s case, reversed: the king of terrors is marched in Christ’s triumphal procession.
15: Nothing of his dwelling remaining, sulfur scattered over his habitation — the complete desolation. The sulfur (gofrit) evokes Sodom and Gomorrah (Genesis 19:24: sulfur and fire from YHWH) as the paradigm of complete divine judgment. Christ took the Sodom-judgment upon himself at Calvary (the “cup” of divine wrath, Mark 14:36) so that those who trust in him escape the desolation. The complete desolation of the wicked’s dwelling is the judgment that fell on Christ at Golgotha.
16: Roots drying up below and branches withering above — the complete plant-death from root to tip. John 15:6: “whoever does not abide in me is thrown away like a branch and withers.” The drying-up from roots describes separation from the source of life. Christ as the true Vine (John 15:1) is the answer to the withered branches of the wicked: those who abide in him are branches that do not wither, precisely because their roots draw from the life that conquered death.
17: Memory of the wicked perishing from the earth, no name remaining in the streets. Psalms 112:6: “the righteous will never be moved; he will be remembered forever.” The contrast is complete: the wicked’s memory perishes; the righteous is remembered forever. Christ’s name has spread to every street of every city of every nation (Acts 1:8: “to the ends of the earth”); the one who was crucified between criminals has the name above every name (Philippians 2:9–11). The perishing memory of the wicked is the anti-type of the eternal name of Christ.
18: Thrust from light into darkness, driven out of the world — the complete spatial displacement of the wicked. Christ’s entry into darkness at the cross was voluntary rather than forced: John 13:30 (“Judas went out; and it was night”) describes the outer darkness that Judas entered; Christ entered that same darkness as the light-bearer who would transform it (John 1:5: “the light shines in the darkness, and the darkness has not overcome it”). The darkness from which the wicked cannot return is the darkness Christ entered and exited in resurrection.
19: No offspring or posterity among his people, no survivor where he sojourned. Isaiah 53:8 asks “as for his generation, who considered that he was cut off out of the land of the living?” and v10 answers: “he shall see his offspring, he shall prolong his days.” The apparent childlessness of the cut-off servant is resolved by the servant’s spiritual offspring: all who receive him he gives “the right to become children of God” (John 1:12). The wicked’s offspring-extinction is the anti-type of the servant who “sees his offspring” in the many sons brought to glory (Hebrews 2:10).
20: Those in the west appalled at the wicked man’s fate, those in the east seized with horror — the universal reverberation of the wicked one’s catastrophic end. The crucifixion similarly reverberated universally: the earthquake (Matthew 27:51–54), the tearing of the temple veil, the centurion’s confession (“truly this was the Son of God!”). The catastrophe at Golgotha sent reverberations east and west that have continued to reach the ends of the earth.
21: “Surely such are the dwellings of the unrighteous, such is the place of him who knows not God” — Bildad’s summary verdict. Ironically, the cross — the place of execution that the Romans designed as the ultimate place of the unrighteous — became the place where God was most fully known. John 12:32: “and I, when I am lifted up from the earth, will draw all people to myself.” The place of the unrighteous became, in Christ’s occupation of it, the place where the unrighteous find God — the ultimate typological reversal.
